_meta.title: Tamil Nadu State Fiscal Data
sources: RBI Handbook of Statistics on Indian States
TVK White Paper on State Finances (June 2026)
DMK White Paper on State Finances (August 2021)
AIADMK White Paper on State Finances (2001)
CAG Reports on State Finances of Tamil Nadu
Economic Survey of Tamil Nadu (P&D Dept)

gsdp.label: GSDP at Current Prices
gsdp.source: MOSPI / TN Directorate of Economics & Statistics
gsdp.help: Gross State Domestic Product — the total value of all goods and services produced in Tamil Nadu in a year. Higher GSDP means a larger, more productive economy.

gsdp_growth.label: GSDP Growth Rate (%)
gsdp_growth.source: MOSPI / TN Directorate of Economics & Statistics
gsdp_growth.help: How fast Tamil Nadu's economy grew compared to the previous year. A positive number means growth; negative means contraction (like during COVID in 2020-21).

per_capita_income.label: Per Capita Net State Income (₹)
per_capita_income.help: Average income per person in Tamil Nadu. This is the NSDP divided by population — a rough measure of individual prosperity.

revenue_receipts.label: Total Revenue Receipts
revenue_receipts.source: TN Finance Department / RBI State Finances
revenue_receipts.help: All money the state government earns — from taxes (like GST, stamp duty, vehicle tax), non-tax sources, and transfers from the central government.

own_tax_revenue.label: State's Own Tax Revenue (SOTR)
own_tax_revenue.help: Taxes collected directly by Tamil Nadu state — SGST, Stamp Duty, Registration, Motor Vehicle Tax, State Excise. This is TN's financial independence measure. TVK White Paper notes SOTR/GSDP fell to a historic low of 5.45%.

sotr_pct_gsdp.label: SOTR as % of GSDP
sotr_pct_gsdp.source: TN Finance Department / TVK White Paper 2026
sotr_pct_gsdp.help: How much of the state's economy is captured as own taxes. A declining ratio means the state is collecting less taxes relative to its economic size — indicating tax administration gaps.

central_transfers.label: Central Tax Devolution + Grants
central_transfers.help: Money Tamil Nadu receives from the central government — its share of national taxes (income tax, GST) plus specific grants. TN's share has been declining as a % of national pool.

revenue_expenditure.label: Revenue Expenditure
revenue_expenditure.help: Day-to-day spending — salaries, pensions, subsidies, interest payments, maintenance. Does NOT create new assets. High revenue expenditure leaves less room for development spending.

capital_expenditure.label: Capital Expenditure
capital_expenditure.help: Investment spending that creates long-term assets — roads, hospitals, schools, infrastructure. Higher capex is generally positive for development. TN's capex has been low compared to debt levels.

interest_payments.label: Interest Payments
interest_payments.help: Amount paid as interest on all outstanding loans. TVK White Paper highlights that interest payments now EXCEED capital expenditure — meaning TN pays more to service past debt than it invests in new assets.

salaries_pensions.label: Salaries & Pensions
salaries_pensions.help: Combined cost of paying all government employees and retired employees. This is the single largest component of committed expenditure — hard to reduce once committed.

subsidies.label: Subsidies & Welfare Transfers
subsidies.help: Spending on free/subsidised schemes — electricity subsidy (TANGEDCO), PDS rice, Amma Canteens, welfare pensions. Popular but can crowd out development spending.

fiscal_deficit.label: Fiscal Deficit
fiscal_deficit.help: The gap between total spending and total income. Government must borrow to fill this gap. Not inherently bad — but if borrowed money funds consumption (not investment), debt grows without economic return.

fiscal_deficit_pct_gsdp.label: Fiscal Deficit as % of GSDP
fiscal_deficit_pct_gsdp.help: FRBM Act mandates fiscal deficit ≤ 3% of GSDP. Tamil Nadu has been breaching this limit consistently. Central govt also allows relaxation during COVID and for capital investment.

revenue_deficit.label: Revenue Deficit
revenue_deficit.help: When day-to-day spending exceeds day-to-day income. A revenue deficit means the state is borrowing even for current expenses — a structural problem that erodes fiscal health.

primary_deficit.label: Primary Deficit
primary_deficit.help: Fiscal deficit minus interest payments. A positive primary deficit means even excluding interest, TN is spending more than it earns — showing underlying fiscal weakness.

debt_stock.label: Outstanding Debt (Direct Liabilities)
debt_stock.source: TN Finance Department / TVK White Paper 2026
debt_stock.help: Total loans the state government owes. Includes market borrowings, loans from Centre, and public account liabilities. Per TVK White Paper (2026), this crossed ₹10 lakh crore.

psu_debt.label: PSU Off-Budget Liabilities
psu_debt.help: Loans taken by state-owned companies (TANGEDCO, TNSTC etc) that don't appear in the state budget but are backed by state guarantees. TVK WP estimates this at ₹3.10 lakh crore.

total_liabilities.label: Total Liabilities (Direct + PSU)
total_liabilities.help: Complete financial obligation of Tamil Nadu including PSU debt. TVK White Paper pegs total liabilities at ₹13.18 lakh crore — every child born in TN carries ₹1.29 lakh of debt.

debt_pct_gsdp.label: Debt as % of GSDP
debt_pct_gsdp.help: How heavy is the debt burden relative to the state's economic size. FRBM guideline: keep below 25%. Tamil Nadu is well above this — and rising.

debt_per_capita.label: Direct Debt Per Capita (₹)
debt_per_capita.help: Each resident's share of state government debt. Does not include PSU liabilities.
